agent_output: {"executive_summary":"For a small courier company (3 engineers) needing reliable, multi-channel delivery notifications with 5-minute latency tolerance, Option B (event-driven with a queue) is the clear winner. The latency budget eliminates the only advantage of synchronous calls, while the queue provides natural retry semantics, channel extensibility, and failure isolation — all without requiring complex infrastructure. A lightweight managed queue (SQS, Cloud Tasks, or even a Postgres-backed job table) keeps operational burden low enough for a small team.","architecture":{"components":[{"name":"Delivery Service","responsibility":"Owns delivery lifecycle. Publishes domain events (DeliveryPickedUp, DeliveryInTransit, DeliveryCompleted, DeliveryFailed) when status changes.","boundaries":"Knows nothing about notifications, channels, or customer contact preferences. Its contract ends at publishing a well-typed event to the queue."},{"name":"Event Queue","responsibility":"Durable, ordered buffer between delivery events and notification dispatch. Provides at-least-once delivery, visibility timeout for retries, and dead-letter queue for poison messages.","boundaries":"Transport only — no business logic. Does not transform or route messages."},{"name":"Notification Worker","responsibility":"Consumes events from the queue, resolves customer contact preferences, selects channels, renders message templates, and dispatches via channel adapters. Handles retries and idempotency.","boundaries":"Owns the notification domain: channel selection, template rendering, dispatch orchestration. Does NOT own delivery domain logic."},{"name":"Channel Adapters","responsibility":"Thin wrappers around external provider APIs (email via SendGrid/SES, SMS via Twilio). Each adapter implements a common interface: send(recipient, message): Result.","boundaries":"One adapter per channel. No business logic — only protocol translation and provider-specific error mapping."},{"name":"Dead Letter Queue","responsibility":"Captures notifications that exhausted all retry attempts for manual inspection and replay.","boundaries":"Operational concern only — alerts on-call engineer, provides replay tooling."}],"interfaces":[{"from":"Delivery Service","to":"Event Queue","contract":"publish(DeliveryStatusEvent { deliveryId, status, timestamp, customerId })","coupling_type":"loose — fire-and-forget via queue, schema coupling only"},{"from":"Event Queue","to":"Notification Worker","contract":"consume() → DeliveryStatusEvent; ack/nack after processing","coupling_type":"loose — queue mediates; worker pulls at its own pace"},{"from":"Notification Worker","to":"Channel Adapters","contract":"ChannelAdapter.send(recipient: ContactInfo, message: RenderedMessage): Result<Success, ChannelError>","coupling_type":"interface coupling — new channels implement the same interface"},{"from":"Channel Adapters","to":"External Providers","contract":"HTTP REST to SendGrid/Twilio/etc.","coupling_type":"external dependency — isolated behind adapter boundary"}],"data_flow":"Delivery Service → [status change] → Event Queue → Notification Worker → resolve customer preferences → select channels → render templates → Channel Adapter(s) → External Provider(s). Failures nack the message back to the queue for retry. After N retries → Dead Letter Queue → alert.","patterns_used":[{"pattern":"Event-Driven Architecture (Option B)","rationale":"The 5-minute latency tolerance makes asynchronous processing viable. Events decouple the delivery domain from notification concerns entirely — the delivery service never blocks on email/SMS providers, and adding new channels requires zero changes to the delivery service."},{"pattern":"Strategy Pattern (Channel Adapters)","rationale":"Each notification channel is a strategy implementing a common send interface. Adding WhatsApp or push notifications means adding a new adapter class — no modification to existing code (Open/Closed Principle)."},{"pattern":"Competing Consumers","rationale":"Multiple notification workers can consume from the same queue for horizontal scaling. Not needed day one, but the pattern is inherent in queue-based architectures — scaling comes free when volume grows."},{"pattern":"Dead Letter Queue","rationale":"Critical for the 'never silently drop' requirement. Messages that fail all retries land in a DLQ for human inspection and replay, providing a safety net without blocking the pipeline."},{"pattern":"Idempotent Receiver","rationale":"At-least-once delivery means duplicate messages are possible. Workers must be idempotent — track processed event IDs to avoid sending duplicate notifications to customers."}]},"domain_model":{"bounded_contexts":[{"name":"Delivery","classification":"core","aggregates":["Delivery (root: deliveryId, tracks status lifecycle, emits DeliveryStatusChanged events)"]},{"name":"Notification","classification":"supporting","aggregates":["NotificationRequest (root: notificationId, tracks channel dispatch attempts and outcomes)","CustomerPreferences (root: customerId, stores contact info and channel preferences)"]},{"name":"Channel Integration","classification":"generic","aggregates":["Adapter configuration per provider (API keys, rate limits, retry policies)"]}],"context_map":[{"upstream":"Delivery","downstream":"Notification","relationship":"Customer-Supplier — Delivery publishes events in its own language; Notification consumes and translates to its own model. Delivery team has no obligation to model notification concerns."},{"upstream":"Notification","downstream":"Channel Integration","relationship":"Anti-Corruption Layer — Channel adapters translate the internal RenderedMessage model into provider-specific API calls, preventing Twilio/SendGrid data shapes from leaking into the notification domain."}]},"trade_offs":[{"decision":"Option B (Event-Driven) over Option A (Synchronous REST)","gains":"Failure isolation: delivery status updates never fail due to email/SMS provider outages. Natural retry via queue visibility timeout. Trivial channel extensibility — add adapter, subscribe to same events. Independent scaling of delivery vs notification paths. Better observability — queue depth is a direct health metric.","sacrifices":"Higher initial complexity vs a simple HTTP call. Requires understanding queue semantics (visibility timeout, DLQ, idempotency). One more infrastructure component to operate (the queue). Slightly harder local development — need queue emulator or in-memory substitute.","alternatives_considered":["Option A: Synchronous REST — simpler day-one but creates tight coupling between delivery updates and notification dispatch. A Twilio outage blocks or degrades delivery status updates. Retry logic must be hand-rolled in the delivery service. Adding new channels means modifying the delivery service. This pattern fights every stated requirement except low latency, which isn't required.","Hybrid: Synchronous with async retry — dispatch synchronously, queue failures for retry. Worst of both worlds: still coupled on the happy path, plus queue complexity for failures. Avoid."]},{"decision":"Managed queue (SQS/Cloud Tasks) over self-hosted (RabbitMQ/Kafka)","gains":"Zero operational overhead for the queue itself — critical for a 3-person team. Built-in DLQ, retry, and monitoring. No cluster management.","sacrifices":"Vendor lock-in to cloud provider. Less control over queue behavior. Cost at very high volumes (unlikely for a courier company).","alternatives_considered":["Postgres-backed job queue (e.g., pg-boss, Graphile Worker) — viable if the team already runs Postgres and wants to avoid adding a new service. Trades queue scalability for operational simplicity. Good option for a small team.","Self-hosted RabbitMQ — too much operational burden for 3 engineers. Reject."]},{"decision":"Single notification worker service (not per-channel microservices)","gains":"One deployable, one codebase, one log stream. Channel routing is internal logic, not infrastructure. Appropriate for team size.","sacrifices":"All channels share a deployment — a bad adapter deploy affects all channels. Acceptable risk at this scale.","alternatives_considered":["Per-channel microservices — overkill for 3 engineers and 2-4 channels. Creates deployment and coordination overhead that outweighs isolation benefits."]}],"flexibility_points":[{"area":"Channel Adapters","evolution_path":"New channels (push, WhatsApp) are added by implementing the ChannelAdapter interface and registering in the channel registry. No changes to event schema, queue, or worker orchestration logic."},{"area":"Event Schema","evolution_path":"New event types (DeliveryDelayed, DeliveryRescheduled) can be published and the notification worker can subscribe selectively. Adding new triggers requires no architectural change."},{"area":"Customer Preferences","evolution_path":"Channel selection logic (which channels per customer, per event type) can evolve independently. Start with 'send all channels' and add preference management later."},{"area":"Queue Technology","evolution_path":"The queue is an infrastructure seam. Swap SQS for Postgres-backed queue or vice versa without touching business logic — only the queue client wrapper changes."}],"rigidity_points":[{"area":"Event Contract (DeliveryStatusEvent schema)","reason":"Intentionally rigid. This is the published language between Delivery and Notification contexts. Changes require versioning discipline. Worth the rigidity — it enforces a clean boundary."},{"area":"At-Least-Once Delivery Semantics","reason":"The entire retry and idempotency design assumes at-least-once delivery. Switching to exactly-once would require rethinking the idempotency layer. This is fine — at-least-once is the right choice."},{"area":"Notification Worker Idempotency","reason":"Must be implemented from day one and maintained rigorously. Every new feature in the worker must preserve idempotency. Non-negotiable for correctness."}],"risks":[{"risk":"Idempotency not implemented correctly, leading to duplicate notifications to customers","severity":"critical","mitigation":"Store processed eventId in a deduplication table with a unique constraint. Check before dispatching. Include idempotency keys in provider API calls where supported (e.g., SendGrid). Write integration tests that simulate redelivery."},{"risk":"DLQ messages ignored, leading to silent notification loss despite infrastructure support","severity":"high","mitigation":"Set up CloudWatch/PagerDuty alerts on DLQ depth > 0. Build a simple replay script from day one. Include DLQ monitoring in the on-call runbook."},{"risk":"Queue poison messages causing worker crash loops","severity":"medium","mitigation":"Set maxReceiveCount on the queue (e.g., 5 retries with exponential backoff). After max retries, message moves to DLQ automatically. Worker should catch all exceptions and nack gracefully rather than crashing."},{"risk":"Provider rate limiting causes cascading retry storms","severity":"medium","mitigation":"Implement per-provider rate limiting in channel adapters. Use exponential backoff with jitter on retries. Monitor provider API response codes and back off on 429s."},{"risk":"Team unfamiliarity with event-driven patterns increases development time","severity":"low","mitigation":"Start with the simplest queue option (Postgres job table or SQS). Avoid Kafka or event sourcing — they solve problems this system doesn't have. Build a working prototype in a spike before committing."}],"implementation_guidance":{"recommended_approach":"Start with a Postgres-backed job queue (pg-boss for Node.js, Procrastinate/Django-Q for Python) if the team already uses Postgres. This avoids adding new infrastructure while providing durable, retryable job processing. Migrate to SQS/Cloud Tasks later if scale demands it. Build the channel adapter interface and two concrete adapters (email, SMS) first. Wire up the delivery service to insert events into the job table on status change. Build one notification worker process that polls the job table, routes to the appropriate adapter(s), and marks jobs complete. Add DLQ alerting from day one.","files_to_create_or_modify":["src/delivery/events/delivery-status-event.ts — Event schema definition","src/notification/worker/notification-worker.ts — Queue consumer and dispatch orchestrator","src/notification/channels/channel-adapter.ts — Common adapter interface","src/notification/channels/email-adapter.ts — Email provider integration","src/notification/channels/sms-adapter.ts — SMS provider integration","src/notification/channels/channel-registry.ts — Maps channel type to adapter instance","src/notification/templates/template-renderer.ts — Message template rendering","src/notification/preferences/customer-preferences.ts — Channel preference resolution","src/notification/idempotency/deduplication-store.ts — Processed event tracking","db/migrations/001_create_notification_jobs.sql — Job queue table","db/migrations/002_create_deduplication_table.sql — Idempotency tracking table"],"patterns_to_follow":"Strategy pattern for channel adapters. Repository pattern for customer preferences and deduplication store. Factory method for creating notification requests from domain events. Keep the worker thin — it orchestrates but delegates rendering, routing, and dispatch to focused collaborators.","pitfalls_to_avoid":["Do NOT build a generic event bus or message broker abstraction — use the queue directly with a thin wrapper. YAGNI.","Do NOT use Kafka. It solves distributed log problems this system does not have, and the operational burden is too high for 3 engineers.","Do NOT retry indefinitely — set a max retry count and use the DLQ. Infinite retries can mask bugs and waste provider API quota.","Do NOT put notification logic in the delivery service. The delivery service publishes events and walks away. Notification is a separate concern.","Do NOT skip idempotency. 'We'll add it later' guarantees duplicate customer notifications in production.","Do NOT build a custom retry/backoff framework. Use the queue's native retry mechanism (visibility timeout + receive count)."]},"escalations":[],"confidence":{"level":92,"high_confidence_areas":["Option B is the correct choice given the stated requirements — the 5-minute latency tolerance, reliability requirement, and extensibility need all point unambiguously to event-driven","Channel adapter strategy pattern is the right extensibility mechanism","Postgres-backed job queue is appropriate for a 3-person team","Idempotency is non-negotiable and must be built from day one"],"low_confidence_areas":["Specific queue technology recommendation depends on the team's existing stack, which is not specified","Template rendering approach (inline vs dedicated service) depends on message complexity requirements not provided"],"assumptions":["The team already operates a Postgres database for delivery data","Notification volume is modest (thousands to low tens-of-thousands per day, typical for a courier company)","The team has basic familiarity with async job processing concepts","Email and SMS providers offer standard REST APIs with retry-safe idempotency keys"]}}
agent_output_excerpt: {"executive_summary":"For a small courier company (3 engineers) needing reliable, multi-channel delivery notifications with 5-minute latency tolerance, Option B (event-driven with a queue) is the clear winner. The latency budget eliminates the only advantage of synchronous calls, while the queue provides natural retry semantics, channel extensibility, and failure isolation — all without requiring complex infrastructure.
